summary: On September 11, 2001, I was at work when we first heard about the Twin Towers going down. Shortly after, we felt a huge vibration and saw smoke near the Pentagon, which was terrifying. That day was the scariest of my life; I was overwhelmed with fear and uncertainty about what would happen next. We had to evacuate the park and cemetery, and I struggled to keep my composure while helping visitors. The whole experience changed how I hear the twenty-one-gun salutes now, as they remind me of that day.

Our location was right next to the Pentagon, so when the attack happened, we felt the impact and saw the huge smoke from the building. I ran outside to see what was going on and was shocked by the devastation. It was surreal and frightening to be so close to such a major event. The chaos around the Pentagon affected everyone nearby, including the people evacuating on foot. That day, the Pentagon was the center of our fear and uncertainty.

entity: Arlington House, The Robert E. Lee Memorial
summary: During the evacuation on September 11, we were told to clear the Arlington House grounds as part of the larger effort to evacuate the Cemetery. Visitors were confused and some even asked if they could still tour the Robert E. Lee Home, which was surreal given the circumstances. We had to close the House and guide people off the premises, all while not having many answers ourselves. It was difficult to maintain order and calm during such an uncertain time.

We helped clear parts of the Cemetery, including near the Tomb of the Unknown Soldier, during the evacuation on September 11. Visitors there were asking questions about what was happening, and I had to tell them we were evacuating without much information to share. It was strange to be responsible for such a solemn place while dealing with the chaos of that day. The Tomb area was part of the perimeter we checked to ensure everyone had left safely.

On September 11, we had to evacuate Arlington National Cemetery after the Pentagon attack. It was a challenging task because we had no clear information and had to keep visitors calm and moving. The Cemetery, usually a place of peace, felt tense and uncertain as we worked with other staff and Tourmobile employees to clear the grounds. I was worried about safety and the unknown threats that day, making it one of the most difficult experiences in my job.

After the evacuation on September 11, I couldn't go home because all the bridges into Washington were shut down, so I stayed in Rosslyn for about four hours. I sat in my car, glued to the radio, trying to figure out the safest place to be amidst the chaos. It was a frightening and lonely time, especially without my family nearby. Rosslyn became a temporary refuge while the city was locked down.

On September 11, the Memorial Bridge and other bridges were shut down, which prevented me from going home. This closure was part of the city's lockdown after the Pentagon attack. The shutdown forced many people, including myself, to stay put in places like Rosslyn. It was eerie to see such major routes closed and to realize the scale of the emergency.

During the evacuation on September 11, I noticed people walking on the Southeast–Southwest Freeway, which was unusual and unsettling. Normally, you wouldn't see pedestrians there, but with the massive evacuation and transportation shutdowns, people were forced to walk on the highway. It highlighted the chaos and fear gripping the area that day.

The Fourteenth Street Bridge was also closed on September 11, which contributed to the difficulty in getting around Washington, D.C. I was stuck in Rosslyn because of these closures and couldn't cross into the city. The bridge shutdowns were part of the security measures after the Pentagon attack and made the day even more stressful.

While I was evacuating and trying to leave the area on September 11, I saw many people walking on streets like Ninth Street in Washington, D.C. It was strange to see so many pedestrians in places where you'd normally expect traffic. This was part of the larger scene of people fleeing the Pentagon area amid the emergency.

Safeway Inc. was mentioned in passing during the day, but I didn't have any direct interaction with them on September 11. My focus was on evacuating the Cemetery and managing visitors. The day was so overwhelming that details about organizations like Safeway didn't register much with me.

Tourmobile employees were also involved in helping evacuate Arlington National Cemetery on September 11. I remember one of the tour guides had a broken foot but was still working to clear visitors. Their cooperation was important in managing the chaos and ensuring everyone left safely. It was a tough day for all staff, including those from Tourmobile.

My parents often talk about historic days they will never forget, like the assassination of Martin Luther King Jr. On September 11, I felt a similar weight of history unfolding. It was a day that felt monumental and deeply frightening, much like those past tragic moments. The comparison helps me understand the lasting impact of that day on my life.

My parents always mention the assassination of John F. Kennedy as a day they will never forget, and for me, September 11 felt just as significant. The fear and uncertainty I experienced reminded me of how people must have felt during those times. It was a historic moment that changed how I view safety and public service. The connection to those past events gives perspective to the gravity of that day.

On September 11, Turkey Run Park staff told us we could leave after the entire park was evacuated. My supervisor coordinated with them to ensure the evacuation was complete before we left. It was part of the larger effort to secure the area after the Pentagon attack. Turkey Run Park played a role in managing the safety of visitors during that chaotic day.
